serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant plate of stir-fried pea shoots, approximately 200 g, as typically served at a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pea shoots, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 11300 adapted for stir-fried preparation with oil); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable entries
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact stir-fried dou miao entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pea shoots (raw) with an adjustment for stir-frying in minimal vegetable oil (~5 g oil per 100 g cooked product), consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable preparation factors. Energy, fat, and saturated fat are adjusted upward from raw values to reflect added oil. Protein, carbohydrate, fibre, and micronutrients are adjusted for ~10% moisture loss during stir-frying. Sodium reflects light seasoning typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables; pea shoots estimated from analogous leafy green vegetables (GI 10–20 range) per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on non-starchy vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried dou miao (pea shoots) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables (e.g., spinach, watercress, choy sum) which consistently fall in the GI 10–20 range in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 4.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4. This is consistent with the very low carbohydrate content of the dish.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 360 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should be aware that restaurant preparations may use more salt than home cooking, and should request reduced seasoning where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried pea shoots have an estimated GI of 15 and provide only approximately 9.6 g of carbohydrate per 200 g serving, making this dish an excellent low-glycemic vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried pea shoots are low in saturated fat (~1 g per serving), contain dietary fibre (~5 g per serving), and provide folate and potassium, all of which support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Dou Miao (清炒豆苗) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish made from the young, tender shoots and leaves of the pea plant (Pisum sativum), known in Mandarin as dou miao. The dish is prepared by rapidly tossing the fresh pea shoots in a very hot wok with a small amount of vegetable oil and salt, a technique known as qing chao (清炒), meaning 'clear stir-fry', which preserves the natural sweetness, vibrant green colour, and delicate texture of the shoots. High-end restaurant versions, such as those served at Din Tai Fung in Singapore, use premium-grade shoots that are uniform in length and quality, and the dish is often prepared without garlic to highlight the pure flavour of the vegetable. The texture is tender yet slightly crisp, with a clean, mildly sweet, and grassy flavour. Nutritionally, stir-fried pea shoots are a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, and low-glycemic vegetable dish, providing approximately 62 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful amounts of protein (3.8 g), dietary fibre (2.5 g), folate, vitamin C, vitamin A, iron, and potassium. The dish is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and those following gluten-free or halal diets. Clinically, it is an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic index (estimated GI 15) and minimal carbohydrate load. The low saturated fat content and high fibre make it beneficial for cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate at restaurant servings and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. Dou miao is widely consumed across Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and mainland China, and is a staple on the menus of Taiwanese-style Chinese restaurants throughout Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: Is dou miao good for diabetics?
Din Tai Fung dou miao calories

confidence_reason: No exact stir-fried dou miao entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pea shoot raw data with cooking adjustments for stir-frying with minimal oil, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA vegetable preparation factors. GI is estimated from analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is well-supported and the food is nutritionally simple.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Vegetables', which is the correct classification for a leafy green vegetable dish. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no exact stir-fried dou miao entry exists in any approved primary source database as of the processing date. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN publishes a direct entry. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pea shoots are a plant food and contain no vitamin B12 by nature (naturally zero). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pea shoots contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central raw pea shoot data. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are present in vegetable oil or pea shoots per USDA data. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pea shoots are a plant food and contain no cholesterol (naturally zero). 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pea shoot raw data with stir-fry preparation adjustment. 8. GI estimated at 15 from analogous non-starchy leafy green vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for dou miao. 9. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a restaurant plate; actual Din Tai Fung portion may vary. 10. The input food_group 'Grains and staples' appears to be a data entry error in the seed record and has been corrected.
